  • Life Expectancy Over Time

Children Facing Multidimensional Deprivation Worldwide

Where and for whom is it worst?

Author

Savia Gomes

Published

April 13, 2026

Introduction

Child deprivation is one of the most pressing global challenges of our time. Across the world, millions of children suffer from at least three simultaneous deprivations — lacking access to education, healthcare, housing, sanitation, or nutrition.

This report uses UNICEF data to explore:

  • Which countries have the highest rates of child deprivation
  • Whether gender plays a role in deprivation levels
  • How economic wealth relates to child deprivation
  • How life expectancy has changed over time in high vs low deprivation countries

Data source: UNICEF Indicator Data & World Bank Metadata

Global Child Deprivation Map

The map below shows child deprivation rates across the world. Sub-Saharan Africa stands out clearly, with several countries exceeding 40% deprivation rates. Grey countries indicate no available data.

Code 
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
from plotnine import *
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

indicator2 = pd.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
metadata = pd.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")
indicator2.columns = indicator2.columns.str.strip()
indicator2 = indicator2.rename(columns={"obs_value(total)": "deprivation_rate", "obs_value(female)": "deprivation_female", "obs_value(male)": "deprivation_male"})
metadata = metadata.rename(columns={"GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)": "gdp_per_capita", "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy", "Population, total": "population"})

url = "https://naturalearth.s3.amazonaws.com/110m_cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
world = gpd.read_file(url)
world_map = world.merge(indicator2[["alpha_3_code", "deprivation_rate"]], left_on="ADM0_A3", right_on="alpha_3_code", how="left")
world_map["deprivation_rate"] = world_map["deprivation_rate"].clip(0, 100)

(
    ggplot(world_map)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="deprivation_rate"), color="white", size=0.1)
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#fff7bc", high="#d73027", na_value="lightgrey")
    + labs(title="Global Child Deprivation Rate (%)", subtitle="Percentage of children suffering at least 3 deprivations", fill="Deprivation %", caption="Source: UNICEF")
    + theme_void()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"))
)

Top 10 Countries with Highest Deprivation

Ethiopia, Madagascar and Chad top the list — all located in Sub-Saharan Africa. These countries face compounding challenges including poverty, conflict, and limited public services.

Code 
top10 = indicator2[["country", "deprivation_rate"]].dropna()
top10 = top10.sort_values("deprivation_rate", ascending=False).head(10)

(
    ggplot(top10, aes(x="reorder(country, deprivation_rate)", y="deprivation_rate"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#d73027")
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(title="Top 10 Countries", subtitle="Percentage of children suffering at least 3 deprivations", x="Country", y="Deprivation Rate (%)", caption="Source: UNICEF")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"))
)

GDP per Capita vs Child Deprivation

There is a clear negative relationship between a country’s wealth and its child deprivation rate. Wealthier nations like Costa Rica maintain very low deprivation, while poorer nations like Ethiopia and Niger struggle with rates above 40%.

Code 
metadata_latest = metadata.sort_values("year").groupby("country").last().reset_index()
scatter_data = indicator2[["country", "deprivation_rate"]].merge(metadata_latest[["country", "gdp_per_capita"]], on="country", how="inner").dropna()
highlight = ["Ethiopia", "Madagascar", "Chad", "Costa Rica", "Guyana", "Niger"]
to_label = scatter_data[scatter_data["country"].isin(highlight)]

(
    ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="deprivation_rate"))
    + geom_point(color="#d73027", size=3, alpha=0.7)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="navy", se=False)
    + geom_text(data=to_label, mapping=aes(label="country"), size=8, nudge_y=2)
    + labs(title="GDP per Capita vs Child Deprivation Rate", subtitle="Higher income countries tend to have lower child deprivation", x="GDP per Capita (constant 2015 US$)", y="Deprivation Rate (%)", caption="Source: UNICEF & World Bank")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"))
)

Life Expectancy Over Time

Countries with high child deprivation consistently show lower life expectancy. Cambodia’s dramatic dip in the late 1970s reflects the impact of the Khmer Rouge genocide. Encouragingly, all countries show an upward trend in recent decades.

Code 
countries = ["Ethiopia", "Madagascar", "Chad", "Costa Rica", "Albania", "Cambodia"]
timeseries_data = metadata[metadata["country"].isin(countries)][["country", "year", "life_expectancy"]].dropna()

(
    ggplot(timeseries_data, aes(x="year", y="life_expectancy", color="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + labs(title="Life Expectancy Over Time by Country", subtitle="Countries with high child deprivation tend to have lower life expectancy", x="Year", y="Life Expectancy (years)", color="Country", caption="Source: UNICEF & World Bank")
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold"))
)

Conclusion

This report highlights the stark global inequalities in child welfare:

  • Sub-Saharan Africa bears the heaviest burden of child deprivation
  • Economic development is strongly linked to lower deprivation rates
  • Gender gaps are minimal — both boys and girls are equally affected
  • Life expectancy is improving globally, but high-deprivation countries still lag significantly behind

Urgent investment in education, healthcare, and economic development in the most affected regions is critical to reversing these trends.
